query: Generate a structured maintenance report for HSM-F3-GBX: current condition, probable fault diagnosis, root cause, remaining useful life and early warning, risk and priority, and recommended immediate and long-term actions.

events.summary: 10 passage(s); top: fault_codes / [[FAULT:F3-GBX-002]] - Rising gear-mesh sidebands with oil iron-particle trend
F3-GBX-002: Rising gear-mesh sidebands with oil iron-particle trend

**Probable fault:** **F3‑GBX‑002 – “Rising gear‑mesh sidebands with oil iron‑particle trend.”**  

**Why this fault matches the current condition**

| Contributing sensor | Observed state (z‑score) | What the fault description expects |
|---------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **vibration_rms_mm_s** | +34.8 z (2.59 mm/s vs baseline 1.20 mm/s) – a large rise in overall RMS vibration. | The fault catalog notes that gear‑mesh‑frequency sidebands appear together with a rise in overall vibration as gear‑tooth damage progresses. |
| **gmf_sideband_db** | +21.3 z (–20.94 dB vs baseline –32 dB) – sideband level is much higher (less negative) than normal. | “Gear‑mesh‑frequency sidebands” are precisely the gmf_sideband metric; an elevated value is the signature of tooth‑level pitting or fracture. |
| **oil_fe_ppm** | +27.99 z (34.6 ppm vs baseline 18 ppm) – ferrous particle count roughly doubled. | The fault defines an “oil iron‑particle trend” rising in step with the sidebands, indicating metal wear from gear‑tooth damage. |
| **oil_temp_C** | +6.3 z (54.8 °C vs baseline 52 °C) – modest temperature rise. | Elevated oil temperature often accompanies increased friction from pitting, supporting the diagnosis but is secondary to the two primary signatures. |

The multivariate anomaly detector flagged these four channels as drivers of the abnormality (score 34.8). The combination of **gear‑mesh sideband increase** *and* **oil Fe‑particle rise** is uniquely described by fault **F3‑GBX‑002** in the fault catalog (see search result [[FAULT:F3-GBX-002]]). No other fault entry couples these two signatures; the generic “gearbox broadband vibration high” (F3‑GBX‑004) lacks the oil‑particle component, and the oil‑over‑temperature code (F3‑GBX‑001) does not mention sidebands.

**Conclusion** – The most specific and highest‑matching fault code is **F3‑GBX‑002**. It directly explains the observed high RMS vibration, elevated gear‑mesh sidebands, and increased ferrous particles in the oil, all pointing to progressing pitting on the Stage‑2 gear set toward tooth fracture. Immediate confirmation via spectrum analysis and oil sampling (per SOP‑GBX‑001, SOP‑OIL‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001) and planning for Stage‑2 gear‑set replacement are therefore recommended.

**Fault code:** F3‑GBX‑002

final.answer:
**Bottom line:** The Stage‑2 gear set in gearbox HSM‑F3‑GBX is developing pitting that is already producing high gear‑mesh sidebands and a doubled iron‑particle count in the oil; the condition is critical and a spare gear set must be ordered **today** with replacement work scheduled **within the next ≈ 2 weeks** to avoid a catastrophic failure.  

---  

## Diagnosis  

- **Observed condition** – Overall vibration has risen to about **2.6 mm/s**, more than double the normal baseline of 1.2 mm/s; the gear‑mesh sideband level is now **–20.9 dB**, markedly higher (less negative) than the typical –32 dB; ferrous particle concentration in the oil is **34.6 ppm**, roughly twice the usual 18 ppm; oil temperature is modestly higher at **54.8 °C** (vs 52 °C) [[diagnostic]].  
- **Fault match** – These signatures exactly match fault **F3‑GBX‑002 – “Rising gear‑mesh sidebands with oil iron‑particle trend”**, which the fault catalog identifies as the hallmark of progressing tooth‑level pitting that can advance to fracture on the Stage‑2 gear set [[FAULT:F3-GBX-002]].  

## Root Cause  

The combined rise of gear‑mesh sidebands and iron‑bearing particles indicates **metal wear on the Stage‑2 gear teeth** – i.e., pitting that is accelerating toward tooth fracture. The modest oil‑temperature increase supports increased friction but is secondary to the primary wear indicators [[diagnostic]].  

## Prediction / Early‑Warning  

| Item | Value | Source |
|------|-------|--------|
| **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** | **≈ 12.3 weeks** until the ISO 10816‑3 damage‑zone (fracture) threshold is reached. Confidence interval **7.4 – 17.3 weeks**. | predictive |
| **Time‑to‑action** | **≈ 1.9 weeks** before the “action” limit is hit. | predictive |
| **Governing degradation channel** | RMS vibration (overall velocity). | predictive |
| **Early‑warning flag** | **TRUE**, severity **high** – triggered by *procurement‑at‑risk* (lower‑bound RUL 7.4 weeks ≤ spare‑part lead time 8 weeks). | risk_priority & predictive |
| **Priority score** | **98.5 / 100**, rank 1, classified as a “vital‑few” asset. | risk_priority |
| **Urgency** | **Critical / Immediate** – spare must be ordered now and repair planned within ≤ 2 weeks. | risk_priority |

The rapid degradation slope (0.023 mm s⁻¹ per day) and the strong multivariate anomaly (score 34.8, severity 0.385) confirm that the observed rise is not ordinary wear but an accelerating fault that will cross the damage‑zone in roughly three months if left unchecked [[predictive]].  

## Recommended Actions  

### 1. Immediate actions (next ≈ 48 h)  

| # | Action | SOP reference | What to do | Why it matters |
|---|--------|---------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Confirm vibration signature** | SOP‑VIB‑001 (ISO 10816) | Install a calibrated triaxial accelerometer on the gearbox housing; run a speed‑sweep and record the gear‑mesh frequency and side‑band amplitudes; compare side‑band RMS to the baseline (< 0.2 mm/s). | Confirms that the side‑band rise truly reflects Stage‑2 tooth pitting (fault F3‑GBX‑002). |
| 2 | **Perform oil sampling & particle analysis** | SOP‑OIL‑001 (ISO 4406) | Take a fresh oil sample from the gearbox sump, send it to the lab for ferrous‑particle count and iron‑specific spectro‑analysis. | Elevated Fe ppm together with side‑bands is the decisive evidence of metal wear. |
| 3 | **Review recent inspection records** | SOP‑GBX‑001 (gearbox inspection) | Check the last four routine inspections (WO‑2026‑0142, WO‑GEN‑0024, WO‑GEN‑0021, WO‑GEN‑0018) to verify that no detailed vibration or oil‑analysis was performed at those times. | Ensures no recent data has been missed and justifies the urgent deep‑dive. |
| 4 | **Create a high‑priority work order for gear‑set replacement** | – | If the vibration and oil results confirm advanced pitting, issue a corrective work order to replace the Stage‑2 gear set (part GBX‑GEAR‑SET‑01). Flag the order as *critical*. | The fault’s consequence is tooth fracture; replacement eliminates the failure mode. |
| 5 | **Confirm spare‑part order status** | – | Contact procurement to verify that **GBX‑GEAR‑SET‑01** is already on‑order (lead ≈ 8 weeks, on‑hand = 0). If not placed, raise an urgent purchase request (expedited shipping if shutdown < 8 weeks). | The lower‑bound RUL (7.4 weeks) is already tighter than the spare‑lead time; any delay would force an unscheduled shutdown. |

### 2. Short‑ to medium‑term monitoring  

| Frequency | Activity | SOP | Acceptance criteria | Comment |
|-----------|----------|-----|---------------------|---------|
| Weekly | Capture vibration trend (GMF side‑bands) | SOP‑VIB‑001 | Side‑band RMS < 0.2 mm/s; > 0.3 mm/s → “Immediate Review”. | Store in CMMS; set automated alarm. |
| Weekly | Oil sampling & Fe‑particle count | SOP‑OIL‑001 | Fe ≤ 10 × 10⁶ µg/L; > 10 × 10⁶ µg/L → “Escalate”. | Compare to the four‑week trend that generated fault F3‑GBX‑002. |
| Monthly | Visual gear‑tooth inspection (borescope) | SOP‑GBX‑001 | No pitting deeper than 0.5 mm. | Detect early pitting before side‑bands rise. |
| Quarterly | Full gearbox inspection (torque, alignment, lubricant viscosity) | SOP‑GBX‑001 | All points within tolerance. | Aligns with normal schedule but adds wear focus. |
| Annually | Review spare‑stock policy | – | Maintain ≥ 1 gear‑set spare for each critical gearbox. | Incorporates lessons from this incident. |

### 3. Spare‑part procurement strategy  

| Item | Current status | Action | Lead time | Responsibility |
|------|----------------|--------|-----------|----------------|
| **GBX‑GEAR‑SET‑01** (Stage‑2 gear set) | On‑order, 0 on‑hand, **8 weeks** lead (OEM B) | Confirm order; if not placed, issue **expedited PO** (air‑freight → ~5 weeks). Negotiate a “critical‑spare” clause with OEM B for priority support. | 8 weeks (standard) → 5 weeks (expedited) | Procurement lead; flag in CMMS. |
| Gear‑set mounting hardware (bolts, lock‑nuts) | Stocked (generic) | Verify ≥ 2 × required set; order extra if < 5 units. | 2 weeks (local vendor) | Maintenance stores. |
| Lubricant (ISO VG‑46, OEM spec) | 1 t ≈ 2 months supply | Reserve 200 L for upcoming change; replenish in next oil‑change cycle. | 3 weeks (warehouse) | Lubrication team. |
| Vibration & oil‑analysis consumables | Sufficient | Perform stock‑check; add to preventive‑maintenance consumable list. | – | Quality‑Control. |

**Key points:**  
1. **One‑gear‑set safety stock** for each critical gearbox.  
2. **Lead‑time monitoring** – weekly PO status; alarm 2 weeks before the 8‑week expiry.  
3. **Alternate supplier scouting** – identify OEM C capable of ≤ 6‑week delivery as a fallback.  
4. **Logistics readiness** – pre‑stage the gear set on‑site, conduct a dimensional pre‑fit before shutdown.  

### 4. Prioritized, actionable roadmap  

| Priority | Recommendation | Evidence |
|----------|----------------|----------|
| **1 (Critical)** | Complete vibration spectrum and oil‑analysis within the next 24 h; if side‑bands > 0.3 mm/s **or** Fe > 10 × 10⁶ µg/L, schedule immediate Stage‑2 gear‑set replacement at the next plant shutdown (≤ 8 weeks). | Fault F3‑GBX‑002 indicates imminent tooth fracture; anomaly score 34.8 and early‑warning high severity [[diagnostic]][[predictive]]. |
| **2** | Verify and, if necessary, place an **expedited purchase order** for GBX‑GEAR‑SET‑01 (lead 8 weeks, critical‑spare clause). | Procurement‑at‑risk trigger (lower‑bound RUL 7.4 weeks ≤ lead 8 weeks) [[risk_priority]]. |
| **3** | Implement weekly vibration and oil‑trend monitoring with CMMS alarms. | Ongoing trend generated the fault; early detection prevents escalation [[predictive]]. |
| **4** | Update spare‑stock policy to hold a complete gear set for every critical gearbox and formalise a “critical‑spare” clause with OEM B. | Priority score 98.5 / 100, rank 1, vital‑few status [[risk_priority]]. |
| **5** | Conduct quarterly comprehensive gearbox inspections (including borescope) per SOP‑GBX‑001. | Aligns inspection cadence with new focus on gear‑wear indicators. |

---  

**Bottom line** – The gearbox is already in a high‑severity, anomalous state driven by advancing pitting. Immediate confirmation (vibration + oil) is required, but the spare part must be ordered **now** and repair work scheduled **within the next ≈ 2 weeks** to stay ahead of the RUL and avoid an unscheduled shutdown. All actions reference the plant SOPs (SOP‑GBX‑001, SOP‑OIL‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001) and the spare‑part record for **GBX‑GEAR‑SET‑01** (on‑order, 8‑week lead).

findings.summary:
**Probable fault:** **F3‑GBX‑002 – “Rising gear‑mesh sidebands with oil iron‑particle trend.”**  

**Why this fault matches the current condition**

| Contributing sensor | Observed state (z‑score) | What the fault description expects |
|---------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **vibration_rms_mm_s** | +34.8 z (2.59 mm/s vs baseline 1.20 mm/s) – a large rise in overall RMS vibration. | The fault catalog notes that gear‑mesh‑frequency sidebands appear together with a rise in overall vibration as gear‑tooth damage progresses. |
| **gmf_sideband_db** | +21.3 z (–20.94 dB vs baseline –32 dB) – sideband level is much higher (less negative) than normal. | “Gear‑mesh‑frequency sidebands” are precisely the gmf_sideband metric; an elevated value is the signature of tooth‑level pitting or fracture. |
| **oil_fe_ppm** | +27.99 z (34.6 ppm vs baseline 18 ppm) – ferrous particle count roughly doubled. | The fault defines an “oil iron‑particle trend” rising in step with the sidebands, indicating metal wear from gear‑tooth damage. |
| **oil_temp_C** | +6.3 z (54.8 °C vs baseline 52 °C) – modest temperature rise. | Elevated oil temperature often accompanies increased friction from pitting, supporting the diagnosis but is secondary to the two primary signatures. |

The multivariate anomaly detector flagged these four channels as drivers of the abnormality (score 34.8). The combination of **gear‑mesh sideband increase** *and* **oil Fe‑particle rise** is uniquely described by fault **F3‑GBX‑002** in the fault catalog (see search result [[FAULT:F3-GBX-002]]). No other fault entry couples these two signatures; the generic “gearbox broadband vibration high” (F3‑GBX‑004) lacks the oil‑particle component, and the oil‑over‑temperature code (F3‑GBX‑001) does not mention sidebands.

**Conclusion** – The most specific and highest‑matching fault code is **F3‑GBX‑002**. It directly explains the observed high RMS vibration, elevated gear‑mesh sidebands, and increased ferrous particles in the oil, all pointing to progressing pitting on the Stage‑2 gear set toward tooth fracture. Immediate confirmation via spectrum analysis and oil sampling (per SOP‑GBX‑001, SOP‑OIL‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001) and planning for Stage‑2 gear‑set replacement are therefore recommended.

**Asset:** HSM‑F3‑GBX **Fault of interest:** F3‑GBX‑002 --- ## 1. Remaining Useful Life (RUL) | Item | Value | Source | |------|-------|--------| | **RUL estimate** | **12.3 weeks** remaining until the ISO 10816‑3 damage‑zone threshold (fracture‑equivalent) is reached. | `predict_rul` result | | **Planning interval** | **[7.4 weeks – 17.3 weeks]** (lower‑/upper‑bound of the 95 % confidence interval). | `predict_rul` result | | **Time‑to‑action (ISO action threshold)** | **≈ 1.9 weeks** before the “action” limit (early‑warning zone) is hit. | `predict_rul` result | | **Governing degradation channel** | **vibration_rms_mm_s** (RMS velocity, mm s⁻¹). | `predict_rul` result | | **Trend method** | **Theil‑Sen robust linear regression** – slope **0.023188 mm s⁻¹ / day** (95 % CI [0.023131, 0.023244]), R² = 0.9883, based on 8 064 samples (04‑Nov 2026 → 06‑Jun 2026). | `predict_rul` result | | **Failure threshold** | **4.5 mm s⁻¹** (ISO 10816‑3 damage‑zone onset). | `predict_rul` result | | **Current vibration level** | **2.498 mm s⁻¹** (well below the threshold but rising). | `predict_rul` result | *Interpretation:* The asset is clearly degrading; at the current rate it will cross the damage‑zone limit in roughly three months, with a fairly tight confidence band. The “action” horizon (≈ 2 weeks) is the interval where maintenance planning should be triggered to avoid crossing into the alert regime. --- ## 2. Anomaly Detection (latest 1 000 samples) | Item | Value | Source | |------|-------|--------| | **Anomaly flag** | **YES** – the recent window is anomalous. | `detect_anomaly` result | | **Anomaly score** | **34.8** (threshold = 6.0). | `detect_anomaly` result | | **Severity (z‑score‑derived)** | **0.385** (0 = normal, 1 = extreme). | `detect_anomaly` result | | **Major contributing channels** | • **vibration_rms_mm_s** (z = 34.8, value = 2.594 mm s⁻¹)
• **oil_fe_ppm** (z = 27.99, value = 34.6 ppm)
• **gmf_sideband_db** (z = 21.31, value = ‑20.94 dB)
• **oil_temp_C** (z = 6.3, value = 54.8 °C) | `detect_anomaly` result | | **ISO‑10816‑3 regime at anomaly** | **alert** (the asset is already in the alert zone for vibration). | `detect_anomaly` result | | **Isolation‑Forest corroboration** | Outlier flag = True (score = ‑0.2405). | `detect_anomaly` result | *Interpretation:* The current degradation is accompanied by a strong multivariate anomaly—especially in vibration, oil‑ferrous‑element content, and GMF sideband—confirming that the observed rise is not just gradual wear but an abrupt shift in condition. --- ## 3. Early‑Warning Assessment | Item | Value | Source | |------|-------|--------| | **Early‑warning verdict** | **TRUE** – the system flags a high‑severity early‑warning. | `assess_early_warning` result | | **Severity** | **high** | `assess_early_warning` result | | **Trigger(s)** | **procurement_at_risk** – the lower‑bound of RUL (7.4 wk) is **≤** the typical spare‑part lead time (≈ 8 wk). | `assess_early_warning` result | | **Recommended action horizon** | **Order spare part immediately**; schedule repair **within the 12.3‑week window** (ideally before the 1.9‑week “action” limit). | `assess_early_warning` result | | **Supporting evidence** | • RUL lower‑bound 7.4 wk ≤ 8‑wk lead time (procurement risk).
• Active anomaly (score 34.8) in the alert regime. | Combined from `predict_rul`, `detect_anomaly`, `assess_early_warning` | *Interpretation:* Because the conservative RUL estimate (7.4 weeks) is already tighter than the time needed to obtain a replacement part, the system raises a **high‑severity early‑warning** and advises **immediate procurement** to avoid unscheduled downtime. --- ## 4. Verdict & Urgency - **RUL:** **≈ 12 weeks** (confidence interval **7–17 weeks**) until the ISO 10816‑3 damage‑zone is reached. - **Early‑warning:** **TRUE**, **high** severity. - **Urgency:** **Immediate** – order the spare part now and plan maintenance within the next **~2 weeks** (the “time‑to‑action” horizon) to stay ahead of the alert regime and avoid catastrophic failure. --- **Bottom line:** The asset is degrading at a robustly quantified rate, has already entered an anomalous alert state, and the lower‑bound RUL is already tighter than the spare‑part lead time. **Take immediate procurement and repair actions** to preserve availability and prevent a catastrophic failure.

findings.summary:
**Asset:** HSM‑F3‑GBX – F3 main‑drive gearbox (herringbone / planetary)  
**Fault code:** F3‑GBX‑002  

---

### 1. Early‑warning risk assessment  
| Item | Result | Comment |
|------|--------|--------|
| **Early‑warning flag** | **TRUE** | The asset is flagged for immediate attention. |
| **Severity** | **high** | Driven by a “procurement‑at‑risk” trigger. |
| **Trigger(s)** | `procurement_at_risk` – RUL lower‑bound 7.4 wk ≤ spare‑lead‑time 8 wk (so the needed part will not arrive before the asset could fail). |
| **Anomaly** | Detected (score 34.8, severity 0.385) on vibration, oil‑temperature, oil‑contamination and gear‑mesh side‑band channels. |
| **RUL** | **≈ 12.3 weeks** (degrading); confidence interval 7.4 – 17.3 wk. |
| **Recommended horizon** | Order the spare now; plan repair within the RUL window (≈ 12 wk). |

*Interpretation*: The gearbox is already showing a measurable degradation, and the spare part needed to fix it will not be in stock when the remaining useful life drops below 8 weeks. This puts the failure risk in the **high** category and demands **immediate** procurement and repair planning.

---

### 2. Maintenance‑priority score (MTR 20/80 methodology)  

| Component | Raw value (from `get_equipment`) | Normalised (0‑1) | Weight | Contribution to score |
|-----------|----------------------------------|------------------|--------|-----------------------|
| **Criticality** | `critical` | **1.00** | 0.40 | **40.0** |
| **Typical delay severity** (min = 420 min) | 420 min | **0.875** | 0.25 | **21.88** |
| **Spare‑availability** | `on_order` | **0.50** | 0.15 | **7.5** |
| **Procurement lead time** | 8 weeks | **0.667** | 0.20 | **13.33** |
| **Dynamic risk** (RUL 12.3 wk + anomaly 0.385) | — | **0.527** | 0.30* (added as a risk overlay) | **15.81** |
| **Base score** (sum of static dimensions) | — | — | — | **82.7** |
| **Risk‑adjusted score** | — | — | — | **98.5** (uncapped) |

*The `compute_priority` call returned a **priority_score = 98.5/100**, rank = 1, and flagged the asset as a **“vital‑few”** (top 2 of 10 assets). No capping was applied.*

---

### 3. Urgency classification  

| Factor | Value | Decision |
|--------|-------|----------|
| **RUL remaining** | 12.3 wk (≈ 84 days) | > 2 weeks but < lead‑time + safety margin |
| **Lead‑time for spare** | 8 wk | Will arrive **after** the low‑bound RUL (7.4 wk) |
| **Early‑warning severity** | high | Immediate action required |

**Urgency = Critical / Immediate** – the spare must be ordered **today** and repair scheduled to start **within the next 2 weeks** (before the 7.4‑week lower‑bound RUL is reached).

---

### 4. Recommended actions (grounded in tool outputs)

| Action | Rationale (tool output) |
|--------|--------------------------|
| **Place an urgent purchase order** for the gearbox‑stage‑2 gear set (spare part) – the `procurement_at_risk` trigger shows the lead‑time (8 wk) exceeds the low‑bound RUL (7.4 wk). |
| **Schedule a maintenance window** within ≈ 2 weeks (the `time_to_action_weeks` from `assess_early_warning` = 1.9 wk). |
| **Prepare for immediate replacement** of the wear‑critical gear set; the anomaly severity (0.385) indicates measurable wear that will accelerate. |
| **Monitor the asset** continuously (vibration, oil quality) until the spare is installed, to catch any rapid deterioration. |
| **Document the fault** (F3‑GBX‑002) in the plant’s reliability database and tag the gearbox as “high‑risk – procurement at risk”. |

---

## Summary  

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| **Early‑warning** | TRUE, **high** severity (procurement‑at‑risk) |
| **Priority score** | **98.5 / 100** (rank 1) |
| **MTR “vital‑few”** | **Yes** (top‑2 of 10 assets) |
| **Urgency** | **Critical – order spare now, repair within ≤ 2 weeks** |
| **Key risk drivers** | RUL ≈ 12 wk, lead‑time 8 wk, anomaly detected on multiple health channels |

The gearbox **must be treated as a plant‑wide priority**; failure would halt the Finishing line and cause a severe production loss far exceeding the typical delay severity (420 min) already built into the priority calculation. Immediate procurement and repair planning are therefore required.

provenance.detail: order spare now; plan repair within RUL ~12.3 wk

findings.summary:
**Equipment:** HSM‑F3‑GBX **Fault:** F3‑GBX‑002 – *Rising gear‑mesh side‑bands together with oil iron‑particle trend* --- ## 1. Immediate (next 48 h) actions | # | Action | SOP reference | What you will do | Why it matters | |---|--------|--------------|------------------|----------------| | 1 | **Confirm vibration signature** | **SOP‑VIB‑001** (vibration measurement, ISO‑10816) | • Install a calibrated triaxial accelerometer on the gearbox housing.
• Run a full speed‑range sweep and capture the gear‑mesh frequency (GMF) and the side‑band amplitudes (GMF ± shaft‑speed harmonics).
• Compare the side‑band RMS to the baseline stored in the condition‑monitoring database (baseline = < 0.2 mm/s). | The fault catalog (F3‑GBX‑002) states that “gear‑mesh‑frequency side‑bands rising together with oil Fe particles” is a strong indicator of **Stage‑2 gear‑tooth pitting** that can progress to fracture. | | 2 | **Perform oil sampling & particle analysis** | **SOP‑OIL‑001** (oil sampling, ISO‑4406) | • Draw a fresh oil sample from the gearbox sump using the approved kit (clean container, filter‑free line).
• Ship to the in‑house lab for ISO‑4406 particle count and iron‑specific spectro‑analysis (Fe µg L‑1).
• Record the current Fe count and compare to the 4‑week trend that triggered the fault. | Elevated Fe particles together with side‑band growth directly confirm metal wear from pitting. | | 3 | **Cross‑check recent inspection records** | **SOP‑GBX‑001** (gearbox inspection) | Review the last four routine inspections (WO‑2026‑0142, WO‑GEN‑0024, WO‑GEN‑0021, WO‑GEN‑0018) – all reported “normal”. Confirm that no detailed vibration or oil analysis was performed at those times. | Ensures no recent missed data point; justifies the urgent deep‑dive. | | 4 | **Issue a “Gear‑Set‑Replacement‑Prepared” work order** | – | • If the vibration/ oil results confirm advanced pitting, create a corrective work order to replace the Stage‑2 gear set (Part GBX‑GEAR‑SET‑01) at the next planned shutdown.
• Flag the work order as *high‑priority* (critical‑severity). | The fault severity is listed as **critical**; immediate replacement prevents catastrophic tooth fracture and unplanned downtime. | | 5 | **Verify spare‑part order status** | – | Call the procurement officer to confirm that the **GBX‑GEAR‑SET‑01** is already on‑order (lead‑time = 8 weeks, on‑hand = 0). If the order has not yet been placed, raise an urgent purchase request (expedited shipping if possible). | A spare must be on‑site before the gear set is removed; the 8‑week lead time leaves a narrow window. | --- ## 2. Longer‑term monitoring & maintenance plan | Frequency | Activity | SOP | Target / Acceptance criteria | Comments | |-----------|----------|-----|-----------------------------|----------| | **Weekly** | Vibration trend capture (GMF side‑bands) | SOP‑VIB‑001 | Side‑band RMS **< 0.2 mm/s**; any > 0.3 mm/s triggers “Immediate Review”. | Store data in the CMMS trend module; set automated alarm. | | **Weekly** | Oil sampling & ISO‑4406 particle count (Fe) | SOP‑OIL‑001 | Fe particle count **≤ 10⁶ µg L‑1**; > 10⁶ µg L‑1 → “Escalate”. | Keep a rolling 4‑week average; compare to the trend that generated fault F3‑GBX‑002. | | **Monthly** | Visual gear‑tooth inspection (borescope) | SOP‑GBX‑001 | No visible pitting > 0.5 mm depth. | Borescope can catch early pitting before side‑bands rise significantly. | | **Quarterly** | Full gearbox inspection (including torque test & alignment) | SOP‑GBX‑001 | All inspection points within tolerance; lubricant viscosity within spec. | Aligns with the normal inspection schedule but adds the extra focus on gear‑wear indicators. | | **Annually** | Review of failure‑trend report & spare‑stock policy | – | Update spare‑part safety‑stock to **≥ 1** gear‑set for critical gearboxes. | Incorporates lessons learned from this incident. | *Thresholds are derived from the fault‑catalog recommendation and the plant’s historical baseline (see vibration baseline in SOP‑VIB‑001).* --- ## 3. Spare‑procurement strategy | Item | Current status | Action | Lead‑time | Responsibility | |------|----------------|--------|----------|-----------------| | **GBX‑GEAR‑SET‑01** (Stage‑2 gear set) | On‑order, 0 on‑hand, **8 weeks** lead (OEM‑B) | • Confirm order placed; if not, issue **expedited PO** (air‑freight if shutdown < 8 weeks).
• Negotiate a **“critical‑spare” clause** with OEM‑B for 1‑year priority support. | 8 weeks (standard) – **5 weeks** if air‑freight. | Procurement lead; maintain a “spare‑on‑site” flag in CMMS. | | **Gear‑set mounting hardware (bolts, lock‑nuts)** | Stocked (generic size) | Verify quantity ≥ 2 × required set; order additional if below 5 units. | 2 weeks (local vendor). | Maintenance stores. | | **Lubricant (ISO‑VG 46, OEM spec)** | On‑hand 1 t – 2 months supply | Reserve **200 L** for the upcoming gear change; replenish during next lubricant‑change cycle. | 3 weeks (warehouse). | Lubrication team. | | **Vibration & oil‑analysis consumables (sensor calibrations, sampling kits)** | Sufficient | Perform a **stock‑check** after this incident; add to preventive‑maintenance consumable list. | – | Quality‑Control. | **Key points of the spare strategy** 1. **One‑gear‑set safety stock** for each critical gearbox (including HSM‑F3‑GBX). 2. **Lead‑time monitoring** – track the PO status weekly; set an alarm 2 weeks before the 8‑week expiry. 3. **Alternate supplier scouting** – query the vendor database for another OEM (e.g., OEM‑C) that can deliver the gear set in ≤ 6 weeks, to be used only if OEM‑B cannot meet the schedule. 4. **Logistics readiness** – pre‑stage the gear set in the on‑site spare‑room, and complete a **pre‑fit inspection** (dimensional check) before the shutdown. --- ## 4. Prioritized, actionable recommendations | Priority | Recommendation | Supporting evidence | |----------|----------------|----------------------| | **1 (Critical)** | Complete **vibration spectrum & oil‑analysis** within the next 24 h (steps 1 & 2). If side‑bands > 0.3 mm/s **or** Fe > 10⁶ µg L‑1, **schedule immediate gear‑set replacement** at the next plant shutdown (≤ 8 weeks). | Fault catalog F3‑GBX‑002 → “Stage‑2 gear‑tooth pitting → fracture” (severity = critical). SOP‑VIB‑001 & SOP‑OIL‑001 prescribe the confirmatory tests. | | **2** | Verify **GBX‑GEAR‑SET‑01** purchase order; if not yet placed, **initiate expedited PO** (air‑freight if shutdown < 8 weeks). | Spare‑part data: on‑hand = 0, lead = 8 weeks, critical for replacement. | | **3** | Implement **weekly vibration & oil‑trend monitoring** (tables 2 & 3) and configure CMMS alarms. | Ongoing trend that generated fault; early detection prevents escalation. | | **4** | Update the **spare‑stock policy** to hold one complete gear set per critical gearbox and formalise a “critical‑spare” clause with OEM‑B. | Prevents repeat situation where the only spare is on‑order. | | **5** | Conduct a **quarterly comprehensive gearbox inspection** per SOP‑GBX‑001, adding the visual borescope step for early pitting detection. | Aligns inspection cadence with the new monitoring focus. | --- ### Bottom line - **Immediate confirmation** of the fault (vibration + oil) is mandatory – it will decide whether gear replacement is required **now**. - **Procurement** of the gear set must be fast‑tracked; an 8‑week lead time leaves limited slack, so an expedited order or alternate vendor should be pursued. - **Monitoring** must be formalised (weekly trends, quarterly inspections) to catch any future side‑band/Fe‑particle growth before it reaches the critical threshold. - **Spare‑stock policy** must be revised to ensure a ready‑to‑install gear set for any critical gearbox, thereby eliminating dependence on a single long‑lead order. All actions reference the documented SOPs (SOP‑GBX‑001, SOP‑OIL‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001) and the spare‑part record for **GBX‑GEAR‑SET‑01** (on‑order, 8‑week lead).
